evidence_quotes.quote: Video games as intellectual property and works of art shouldn't be lost to time because of the negligence of the people behind the games.
It creates a FOMO (fear of missing out) situation to squeeze more money from consumers.

In these games, Battle Pass gives nothing of value to players if they don't buy a higher tier on a subscription service, where if you don't play every day, you won't be able to finish that battle pass due to lack of time, so you won't get your moneys worth.
For some reason, almost every game that launches has "tiers" or "deluxe editions" that sometimes add nothing more but cosmetics and nothing that influences the gameplay.
Most shops selling video games are actually selling licenses for them.
It makes it, so consumers don't actually own what they buy, and publishers can revoke the license whenever they want.

paragraphs.text: - Deleting games from stores and also from libraries of people that have already bought them. In situations like shutting down servers or games are too old. Even after that, games should be available somewhere. Video games as intellectual property and works of art shouldn't be lost to time because of the negligence of the people behind the games. - Early acces to games and the ability to patch games after publishing gave publishers too much liberty. It causes situations where consumers buy a game, sometimes for a higher price for early access, with a promise from the publisher to finish the game or fix the game after launch. There is no consequence of not delivering on that promise, sometimes leaving consumers with games that are made really quickly and cheaply, trying to fool consumers into buying games that are abandoned early after launch. - For some reason, almost every game that launches has "tiers" or "deluxe editions" that sometimes add nothing more but cosmetics and nothing that influences the gameplay. Usually these editions come with a few days of access to a game before launch. It creates a FOMO (fear of missing out) situation to squeeze more money from consumers. - Gambling in games and including Gacha games that, in some circles, are not considered gambling. We have already been through a situation with lootboxes. Gachas are the same, just a different package. - After lootboxes, we also get Battle Passes that, in my opinion, are mostly done badly. In these games, Battle Pass gives nothing of value to players if they don't buy a higher tier on a subscription service, where if you don't play every day, you won't be able to finish that battle pass due to lack of time, so you won't get your moneys worth. - Most shops selling video games are actually selling licenses for them. It makes it, so consumers don't actually own what they buy, and publishers can revoke the license whenever they want. Sometimes it happens to an older video game to force consumers to buy new ones. - People should have a right to buy anything that they want (anything legal, of course) and shouldn't be stopped by credit card providers. Same with social media. Blocking, deleting "shadow-banning" etc. posts on social media, art, video games is just censorship, no matter what it is, and is against freedom of speech. And if we as a government need to help children to be safe on the internet, then it is a problem with parents of these children, who can't take care of them properly. The Internet, as a technology, has made everything more accessible. It is a convenient technology. Nowadays, companies are going against that convenience to earn as much money from consumers as they can legally use different techniques, trying to hide their intentions. People are going back to piracy because it has again become more convenient than legally buying a product. If buying a product is not owning it, then pirating is not stealing. Fixing the situation is about bringing back convenience for consumers.
